 {"id":"git_safety_guard-5apz.1","title":"[Suggest] Risk scoring + explicit manual confirmation","description":"# Task: Risk scoring + explicit manual confirmation

## Purpose
Suggestions must never auto-write allowlist entries. Each suggestion should include
an explicit risk score and require manual confirmation before writing.

## Requirements
- Risk scoring independent from confidence scoring
- Risk categories: low / medium / high (deterministic rules)
- Interactive CLI must require explicit confirmation per entry
- Non-interactive mode outputs suggestions only (no writes)

## Implementation
- Add `risk_score` to suggestion model
- Define scoring rules (flags like `--force`, destructive verbs, system paths)
- Enforce confirmation in CLI flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- No allowlist entry is written without a user confirmation step
- Risk score is visible in both text and JSON outputs

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-5hz9","title":"Improve span classification to recognize string literal contexts","description":"## Objective

Enhance the context/span classification system to properly identify and handle string literal contexts where dangerous patterns should not trigger denial.

## Context Types to Handle

1. Single-quoted strings: 'dangerous text here'
2. Double-quoted strings: "dangerous text here"  
3. Dollar-quoted strings: $'...'
4. Heredocs: <<EOF ... EOF
5. Command arguments that are clearly data, not code

## Implementation Approach

1. Review classify_command in src/context.rs
2. Add new span kinds for string literal content
3. Ensure pattern matching only runs against executable spans
4. Add tests for each context type

## Acceptance Criteria

- String literals correctly classified as non-executable
- Pattern matching respects span classification
- No regression in detecting actual dangerous commands
- Tests cover all string context types","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T00:14:58.707446109-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-16T00:39:21.703171383-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-16T00:39:21.703171383-05:00","close_reason":"Completed as part of heredoc false positive fix","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-5hz9","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1x7y","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-16T00:15:51.268400593-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}

-{"id":"git_safety_guard-5rbb.9","title":"[Scan] Add SARIF 2.1.0 output format","description":"# Task: Add SARIF 2.1.0 Output Format for Scan Mode

## Background

SARIF (Static Analysis Results Interchange Format) is the industry standard for security scanning output. It integrates natively with:
- GitHub Code Scanning
- GitLab Code Quality
- Azure DevOps
- VS Code SARIF Viewer extension
- SonarQube

Adding SARIF support positions DCG as a first-class citizen in CI/CD security pipelines.

## Implementation

### CLI Integration

```
dcg scan . --format sarif > results.sarif
dcg scan . --format sarif --output results.sarif
```

### SARIF 2.1.0 Structure

```json
{
  "$schema": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/oasis-tcs/sarif-spec/main/sarif-2.1/schema/sarif-schema-2.1.0.json",
  "version": "2.1.0",
  "runs": [{
    "tool": {
      "driver": {
        "name": "dcg",
        "version": "2.0.0",
        "informationUri": "https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard",
        "rules": [
          {
            "id": "git.force-push",
            "name": "Force Push Detection",
            "shortDescription": { "text": "Detects git push --force" },
            "fullDescription": { "text": "..." },
            "defaultConfiguration": { "level": "error" },
            "helpUri": "https://dcg.dev/rules/git/force-push"
          }
        ]
      }
    },
    "results": [
      {
        "ruleId": "git.force-push",
        "level": "error",
        "message": { "text": "Destructive git force push detected" },
        "locations": [{
          "physicalLocation": {
            "artifactLocation": { "uri": ".github/workflows/ci.yml" },
            "region": {
              "startLine": 45,
              "startColumn": 9,
              "endLine": 45,
              "endColumn": 35
            }
          }
        }],
        "fixes": [{
          "description": { "text": "Remove --force flag" },
          "artifactChanges": [{
            "artifactLocation": { "uri": ".github/workflows/ci.yml" },
            "replacements": [{
              "deletedRegion": { "startLine": 45, "startColumn": 20, "endColumn": 28 },
              "insertedContent": { "text": "" }
            }]
          }]
        }]
      }
    ]
  }]
}
```

### Key Files

- src/sarif.rs - New: SARIF output formatting
- src/scan.rs - Add sarif format option
- src/cli.rs - Wire up --format sarif

### Rust Implementation

```rust
// src/sarif.rs
use serde::Serialize;

#[derive(Serialize)]
pub struct SarifReport {
    #[serde(rename = "$schema")]
    pub schema: String,
    pub version: String,
    pub runs: Vec<SarifRun>,
}

#[derive(Serialize)]
pub struct SarifRun {
    pub tool: SarifTool,
    pub results: Vec<SarifResult>,
}

impl SarifReport {
    pub fn from_scan_results(findings: Vec<ScanFinding>) -> Self {
        // Convert DCG findings to SARIF format
    }
}
```

### Severity Mapping

| DCG Severity | SARIF Level | Security Score |
|--------------|-------------|----------------|
| critical     | error       | 9.0-10.0       |
| high         | error       | 7.0-8.9        |
| medium       | warning     | 4.0-6.9        |
| low          | note        | 0.1-3.9        |

## GitHub Actions Integration

```yaml
name: Security Scan
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  dcg-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Install DCG
        run: curl -fsSL https://dcg.dev/install.sh | bash
      
      - name: Run DCG scan
        run: dcg scan . --format sarif --output results.sarif
      
      - name: Upload SARIF
        uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3
        with:
          sarif_file: results.sarif
```

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (tests/sarif_test.rs)

```rust
#[test]
fn test_sarif_schema_compliance() {
    log::info\!("=== Testing SARIF 2.1.0 schema compliance ===");
    
    let findings = vec\![ScanFinding::mock_high_severity()];
    let sarif = SarifReport::from_scan_results(findings);
    let json = serde_json::to_string_pretty(&sarif).unwrap();
    
    log::debug\!("Generated SARIF:\n{}", json);
    
    // Validate against schema
    let schema = load_sarif_schema();
    let instance: serde_json::Value = serde_json::from_str(&json).unwrap();
    let result = schema.validate(&instance);
    
    assert\!(result.is_ok(), "SARIF must validate against schema");
    log::info\!("=== SARIF schema compliance test PASSED ===");
}

#[test]
fn test_sarif_severity_mapping() {
    log::info\!("=== Testing severity to level mapping ===");
    
    let mappings = [
        (Severity::Critical, "error"),
        (Severity::High, "error"),
        (Severity::Medium, "warning"),
        (Severity::Low, "note"),
    ];
    
    for (severity, expected_level) in mappings {
        let finding = ScanFinding::with_severity(severity);
        let sarif = SarifReport::from_scan_results(vec\![finding]);
        let level = &sarif.runs[0].results[0].level;
        
        log::debug\!("Severity {:?} -> Level {}", severity, level);
        assert_eq\!(level, expected_level);
    }
    
    log::info\!("=== Severity mapping test PASSED ===");
}
```

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/sarif_e2e.sh)


```rust
    log::info\!("=== Testing SARIF 2.1.0 schema compliance ===");
    
    let findings = vec\![ScanFinding::mock_high_severity()];
    let sarif = SarifReport::from_scan_results(findings);

}

#[test]
fn test_sarif_severity_mapping() {
    log::info\!("=== Testing severity to level mapping ===");
    
    let mappings = [
        (Severity::Critical, "error"),
        (Severity::High, "error"),
        (Severity::Medium, "warning"),
        (Severity::Low, "note"),
    ];
    
    for (severity, expected_level) in mappings {
        let finding = ScanFinding::with_severity(severity);
        let sarif = SarifReport::from_scan_results(vec\![finding]);
        let level = &sarif.runs[0].results[0].level;
        
        log::debug\!("Severity {:?} -> Level {}", severity, level);
        assert_eq\!(level, expected_level);
    }
    
    log::info\!("=== Severity mapping test PASSED ===");
}
```

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-64dc","title":"[PERF][P0] Startup latency: lazy pack registry + lazy regex compilation","description":"# Goal

Make the **common allow path** (commands that should pass quickly, e.g. `ls`, `cd`, `rg`) fast again by eliminating the current dominant startup cost: eager pack construction + eager regex compilation.

This is **pure performance work**. It must be **provably isomorphic**: for the same input JSON, the allow/deny decision (including `pack_id`, `pattern_name`, reason strings, fail-open behavior, and ordering/tie-breaking) must not change.

# Why this matters (real numbers)

dcg runs once per Bash command. If the hook costs ~175ms, that is catastrophic: it makes every command feel sluggish.

Baseline (measured 2026-01-09, using `./target/release/dcg`):

## Correctness baseline
- `cargo test` → pass
- `cargo build --release`
- `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --binary ./target/release/dcg` → **pass** (211/211)

## Process-per-command latency baseline (warmup 30, measure 300)
Measured by looping the release binary and piping hook JSON on stdin.

- `DCG_BYPASS=1` (returns after config load, *before packs*):
  - p50 ≈ **1.55ms**, throughput ≈ **643/s**

- Normal hook allow/deny cases (`ls`, `git status`, deny cases, heredoc-ish cases):
  - p50 ≈ **173–179ms**, throughput ≈ **5.6–5.8/s**

Key isolate:
- `./target/release/dcg config`: p50 ≈ **1.58ms**
- `./target/release/dcg packs --enabled`: p50 ≈ **177.5ms**

This strongly implies: **pack registry initialization dominates**.

## Peak memory baseline
Using `/usr/bin/time -v`:
- Hook run: Max RSS ≈ **15.5MB**
- `DCG_BYPASS=1`: Max RSS ≈ **4.7MB**
- `dcg packs --enabled`: Max RSS ≈ **14.0MB**

# Profile before proposing (done; top hotspots)

`perf` was blocked on this host (`perf_event_paranoid=4`), so profiling used **gprofng**.

CPU + heap profiles show:
- `destructive_command_guard::packs::PackRegistry::new` dominates.
- Inside it, `fancy_regex::Regex::new` + compilation/determinization dominates.
- Heap tracing shows extremely high allocation volume in regex compilation.

Artifacts captured under `/tmp` (not in repo):
- `/tmp/dcg_packs_loop_cpu.er`
- `/tmp/dcg_packs_debug.er`

# Root cause (current architecture)

`REGISTRY` initializes a `PackRegistry` that constructs **all packs** and eagerly compiles **all regexes** (including packs that are not enabled for the current run, and even when the command is quick-rejectable).

This makes even trivial allow commands pay the full regex compilation cost.

# Strategy (high-level)

1. **Make pack registry metadata-only** (pack ids, tier, keywords, pattern *strings*).
2. **Defer regex compilation until actually needed**:
   - Parse hook JSON to get `command` first.
   - Run keyword gate using metadata-only keyword sets.
   - Only if needed: compile regexes for the enabled packs that might match.
3. Keep semantics isomorphic:
   - Pack ordering remains stable.
   - Safe-before-destructive logic unchanged.
   - Allowlists and config overrides unchanged.
   - Fail-open behavior unchanged.

# Success criteria / perf budgets (post-change targets)

These targets are intentionally aggressive because current overhead is dominated by avoidable work.

- `dcg` hook allow case (no enabled pack keywords):
  - p50 ≤ **5ms**, p95 ≤ **10ms**, p99 ≤ **20ms** (release build)

- `dcg packs --enabled`:
  - p50 ≤ **5ms** (should be metadata-only)

- Max RSS:
  - allow case ≤ **6MB**

# Regression guardrails

- Add tests that assert "no regex compilation on quick-reject allow path" (via a `#[cfg(test)]` compile counter in the regex engine).
- Add a repeatable perf harness that records p50/p95/p99 + throughput and stores JSON artifacts in-repo for diffing.
- CI should fail if allow-path budgets regress.

# Non-goals

- Changing policies (WARN vs DENY) or reducing false positives by altering rules.
- Introducing daemons or changing operational semantics.
- Broad refactors not directly tied to startup/compile elimination.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.1","title":"[DESIGN] PackSpec + LazyPattern architecture (no eager regex compile)","description":"# Objective

Design an internal representation for packs/patterns that allows:

- Listing packs and keywords **without compiling regex**.
- Running the hook allow path (quick reject) **without compiling regex**.
- Compiling regex **only** for the enabled packs that are actually evaluated.

This must be **isomorphic** to current behavior.

# Background / evidence

Baseline + profiling show startup is dominated by:
- `packs::PackRegistry::new`
- `fancy_regex::Regex::new` compilation

Even `dcg packs --enabled` costs ~177ms (release) because it triggers registry construction.

# Constraints (must preserve)

- Pack ordering + tie-breaking remains deterministic (existing stable order behavior).
- Safe-before-destructive evaluation is preserved.
- `pack_id`/`pattern_name` attribution remains stable.
- Fail-open semantics remain unchanged.
- No new false positives/negatives.

# Proposed representation

## Pack metadata (`PackSpec`)

A `PackSpec` must contain only:
- `pack_id: &'static str`
- `tier: Tier` (or ordering key)
- `keywords: &'static [&'static str]`
- `safe_patterns: &'static [SafePatternSpec]`
- `destructive_patterns: &'static [DestructivePatternSpec]`
- Any pack-specific knobs that are strictly metadata (no compiled state)

## Pattern specs (store *strings*, compile lazily)

### `SafePatternSpec`
- `name: &'static str`
- `pattern: &'static str`
- `compiled: OnceLock<CompiledRegex>` (or `OnceLock<fancy_regex::Regex>` initially)

### `DestructivePatternSpec`
- `name: Option<&'static str>`
- `pattern: &'static str`
- `reason: &'static str`
- `compiled: OnceLock<CompiledRegex>`

API sketch:
- `fn is_match(&self, haystack: &str) -> bool`
- `fn find_match_span(&self, haystack: &str) -> Option<(usize, usize)>` (needed for explainability / matched-span output)

Compilation behavior:
- Compiled on first use (per-process), using `OnceLock::get_or_init`.

# Handling regex compilation errors (isomorphism considerations)

Current code uses `.expect("pattern should compile")` at pack construction time, which would crash early if a pattern is invalid.

With lazy compilation, compile failures would surface on first use. This changes *when* the panic happens (not the allow/deny output on valid builds).

To keep "invalid patterns are caught immediately" as a property **without paying compile cost in production**:

- Add a **unit test** `all_pack_patterns_compile` that compiles every pattern once (under `#[cfg(test)]`).
- Optionally add `dcg packs --validate` to compile everything explicitly (debug-only / developer command).

This maintains strong correctness while still letting production avoid compilation on the quick allow path.

# Where the architecture must integrate

- `src/main.rs` hook path must be able to compute:
  - enabled pack ids
  - keyword union
  - pack order

…without forcing regex compilation.

- `dcg packs --enabled` must list packs without compilation.

# Acceptance criteria

- No `Regex::new` calls occur during registry init for allow-case commands.
- `dcg packs --enabled` becomes metadata-only and fast.
- Design explicitly documents:
  - which modules/structs change
  - how deterministic ordering is preserved
  - how match spans are extracted without double-compiling

# Deliverable

A short design note (can live directly in this bead as updates/comments) that commits to:
- concrete types (`PackSpec`, pattern spec structs, lazy compile primitive)
- migration plan (phase-by-phase)
- explicit isomorphism proof sketch

## Design

Progress indicators for:
1. File scanning (progress bar with file count)
2. Pattern loading (spinner during startup)
3. History analysis (progress for suggest-allowlist)

## Implementation

```rust
use indicatif::{ProgressBar, ProgressStyle, MultiProgress};

pub struct ScanProgress {
    bar: ProgressBar,
}

impl ScanProgress {
    pub fn new(total_files: u64) -> Self {
        let bar = ProgressBar::new(total_files);
        bar.set_style(
            ProgressStyle::default_bar()
                .template("{spinner:.green} [{bar:40.cyan/blue}] {pos}/{len} files ({eta})")
                .unwrap()
                .progress_chars("█▓░")
        );
        Self { bar }
    }
    
    pub fn tick(&self, file_path: &str) {
        self.bar.set_message(file_path.to_string());
        self.bar.inc(1);
    }
    
    pub fn finish_with_message(&self, msg: &str) {
        self.bar.finish_with_message(msg.to_string());
    }
}

pub fn spinner(message: &str) -> ProgressBar {
    let spinner = ProgressBar::new_spinner();
    spinner.set_style(
        ProgressStyle::default_spinner()
            .tick_strings(&["⠋", "⠙", "⠹", "⠸", "⠼", "⠴", "⠦", "⠧", "⠇", "⠏"])
            .template("{spinner:.blue} {msg}")
            .unwrap()
    );
    spinner.set_message(message.to_string());
    spinner
}
```

## Integration Points

- src/scan.rs - Wrap file iteration with progress
- src/suggest.rs - Progress for history analysis
- src/main.rs - Spinner during config loading (if slow)

## Threshold for Progress

Only show progress bar when:
- Scanning >20 files (otherwise too fast to see)
- Operation takes >500ms

```rust
fn e2e_long_scan_shows_progress() {
    log::info\!("=== E2E: Testing progress bar for long scans ===");
    
    // Create directory with many files
    let temp_dir = create_large_test_directory(150); // 150 files
    
    let start = Instant::now();
    let child = Command::new("dcg")
        .args(["scan", temp_dir.path().to_str().unwrap()])
        .stdout(Stdio::piped())
        .spawn()
        .unwrap();
    
    // Read output incrementally
    let mut reader = BufReader::new(child.stdout.unwrap());
    let mut saw_progress = false;
    let mut line = String::new();
    
    while reader.read_line(&mut line).unwrap() > 0 {
        log::trace\!("Output line: {}", line.trim());
        if line.contains("%") || line.contains("█") || line.contains("Scanning") {
            saw_progress = true;
        }
        line.clear();
    }
    
    let duration = start.elapsed();
    log::info\!("Scan completed in {:?}, progress shown: {}", duration, saw_progress);
    
    assert\!(saw_progress, "Progress indicator should appear for large scans");
    
    log::info\!("=== E2E: Progress bar test PASSED ===");
}
```

```rust
    let mut reader = BufReader::new(child.stdout.unwrap());
    let mut saw_progress = false;
    let mut line = String::new();
    
    while reader.read_line(&mut line).unwrap() > 0 {
        log::trace\!("Output line: {}", line.trim());
        if line.contains("%") || line.contains("█") || line.contains("Scanning") {
            saw_progress = true;
        }
        line.clear();
    }
    
    let duration = start.elapsed();
    log::info\!("Scan completed in {:?}, progress shown: {}", duration, saw_progress);
    
    assert\!(saw_progress, "Progress indicator should appear for large scans");

}
```

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-advk.9","title":"[TUI] Interactive confirmation prompts for dangerous commands","description":"# Task: Interactive Confirmation Prompts for Dangerous Commands

## Background

When DCG blocks a command interactively (not in CI/AI agent mode), it should offer the user a clear choice rather than just denying. The inquire crate provides beautiful, accessible prompts.

## Example Interaction

```
╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│  ⚠️  Potentially Destructive Command Detected                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  $ git push --force origin main                               │
│                                                               │
│  Pattern: git.force-push-main                                 │
│  Risk: Overwrites remote history, may affect collaborators    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  What would you like to do?                                   │
│                                                               │
│  > ○ Block this command (default)                             │
│    ○ Allow once (this time only)                              │
│    ○ Add to allowlist (remember for future)                   │
│    ○ Show more details                                        │
╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
```

## Implementation

### Dependencies

```toml
inquire = "0.7"  # Beautiful interactive prompts
```

### Prompt Logic

```rust
use inquire::Select;

pub fn prompt_for_action(pattern: &PatternMatch) -> Action {
    // Skip prompts in non-interactive contexts
    if \!std::io::stdin().is_terminal() || env::var("DCG_NON_INTERACTIVE").is_ok() {
        return Action::Block;
    }
    
    let options = vec\![
        "Block this command (recommended)",
        "Allow once",
        "Add to allowlist",
        "Show more details",
    ];
    
    let answer = Select::new("What would you like to do?", options)
        .with_help_message("Use arrow keys to select, Enter to confirm")
        .prompt();
    
    match answer {
        Ok("Allow once") => Action::AllowOnce,
        Ok("Add to allowlist") => Action::AddToAllowlist,
        Ok("Show more details") => Action::ShowDetails,
        _ => Action::Block,
    }
}
```

### When to Show Prompts

1. TTY detected (stdin is terminal)
2. Not in CI (no CI=true env var)
3. Not run by AI agent (no hook mode JSON output expected)
4. Severity is medium or below (critical always blocks without prompt)

### Non-Interactive Fallback

```rust
// Environment variable to force non-interactive
if env::var("DCG_NON_INTERACTIVE").is_ok() {
    return default_action_for_severity(pattern.severity);
}
```

- Colors render properly
- Non-TTY contexts skip prompts
- CI environments skip prompts
- Default action is safe (block)
- Allow once does not persist
- Add to allowlist persists correctly

## Accessibility

- Works with screen readers (inquire supports this)
- Keyboard-only navigation
- Clear contrast (uses theme system)

## Testing

- Test with actual terminal
- Test with piped input (should auto-block)
- Test with CI=true env var

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-ahm","title":"BUG: Config precedence cannot override back to defaults","description":"## Problem

Config layering claims "other takes priority" (env > project > user > system > defaults), but current `Config::merge` uses sentinel/monotonic merges that prevent higher-priority layers from explicitly setting values back to defaults.

Examples observed in `src/config.rs` merge logic:
- `general.verbose` can only ever become `true`; cannot be set back to `false`.
- `general.color` cannot be set back to `auto`.
- `logging.enabled` can only ever become `true`; cannot be disabled.
- `logging.format` cannot be set back to `text`.
- `logging.events` filters are one-way toggles (deny/warn only disable; allow only enable).

This breaks the mental model of config precedence, makes behavior hard to reason about, and can cause persistent "can't turn it off" UX footguns that feel like false positives / velocity killers (especially verbose/logging output).

## Root cause

Config files deserialize into fully-defaulted structs (`#[serde(default)]`), losing information about whether a field was absent vs explicitly set to the default value. Merge currently approximates presence by comparing to default/sentinel values, which fails for "explicit default" cases.

## Goal

Make config layering **presence-aware** so that higher-precedence configs can explicitly set any supported value (including the default value) and have it win deterministically.

## Proposed approach

- Parse file configs into a "layer"/"partial" representation where scalar fields are `Option<T>` so we preserve field presence.
- Merge layers into the effective `Config` by applying only fields that are `Some(...)`.
- Keep existing additive semantics for list/map sections (packs/rules) unless explicitly re-scoped.

## Acceptance criteria

- If system config sets `general.verbose=true` and user config sets `general.verbose=false`, effective config is `false`.
- If system config sets `general.color="never"` and user config sets `general.color="auto"`, effective config is `auto`.
- If system config enables logging and user config disables it, effective config disables.
- Similar reversibility for `logging.format`, redaction, and event filters.

## Tests

Add unit tests that construct two TOML layers (system + user) and verify precedence for the above cases. Prefer tests that parse TOML into the new layer type to ensure real-world behavior.
